metadata.summary: Time-boxed process (1–4 weeks) for de-risking an idea before committing build resources, testing value, usability, feasibility, and business viability risks.

content.title: Product Discovery
content.summary: Product discovery is a time-boxed process (1–4 weeks) for de-risking an idea before committing build resources. It tests four risk types — value (will customers buy/use it?), usability (can they figure it out?), feasibility (can we build it?), and business viability (does it work for the business?) — using the cheapest technique that gives sufficient confidence at each stage.

why.p: Teams that skip discovery build features nobody wants. Marty Cagan's research shows that roughly half of features shipped by product teams deliver zero measurable value — not because of poor execution but because the wrong things were built. Discovery is cheap compared to the engineering cost of a feature that fails post-launch.

list.item: Pre-build validation of any significant feature or product idea.
High-risk assumptions that would kill the idea if wrong (value risk, feasibility risk).
Before committing to a 4+ week development effort.
When the team disagrees on whether users actually have the problem being solved.
Tiny bug fixes or incremental improvements with known user impact — just ship.
Compliance or security requirements with no alternative — no discovery needed, build it.
Post-PMF continuous learning — use continuous-discovery instead (recurring cadence vs one-time deep dive).
Discovery that would take longer than the build itself — run a spike instead.

rule.statement: Test risks in this order of priority: value risk first, then usability, then feasibility, then business viability. Kill ideas early on value risk before spending on later-stage tests.
rule.rationale: Value risk is the most common and cheapest to test. Building a technically feasible, usable product that nobody wants is the most expensive failure mode in product development.

rule.statement: Allocate 20–30% of team capacity to discovery. Never skip to delivery without addressing the highest-risk assumption.
rule.rationale: The cost of a failed feature is the engineering time plus the opportunity cost of what was not built instead. 20% discovery investment that prevents one failed 6-week feature pays for itself many times over.

list.item: Customer interviews — low effort, medium confidence — use for early-stage ideas
Fake door test (link to feature that does not exist yet) — low effort, medium confidence — use for testing demand
Landing page with signup — low effort, medium confidence — use for market interest
Concierge MVP (deliver value manually) — medium effort, high confidence — use for service-based products
Wizard of Oz (fake automation) — medium effort, high confidence — use for complex features
Paper prototypes — low effort, low confidence — use for early concept validation
Clickable prototypes — medium effort, medium confidence — use for flow validation
Usability testing with 5 users — medium effort, high confidence — use for design validation
A/B testing — high effort, very high confidence — use for optimization, not validation
Technical spike (1–2 days) — low effort, high confidence — use for unknown tech
Architecture review — low effort, medium confidence — use for system impact assessment
Proof of concept — medium effort, high confidence — use for critical path features
Financial modeling — low effort, medium confidence — use for revenue impact
Competitive analysis — low effort, medium confidence — use for market fit
Pilot program — high effort, very high confidence — use only for major investments

rule.statement: List every assumption the idea depends on before choosing a technique. Categorize each by risk type (value/usability/feasibility/business) and risk level (high/medium/low).
rule.rationale: Undiscovered assumptions are the primary source of post-launch surprises. An explicit assumption list makes the team agree on what they are betting on before committing resources.

rule.statement: Focus discovery on assumptions that are both high-risk AND easy to test. High-risk and hard-to-test assumptions need a spike or POC first to reduce uncertainty before running user experiments.
rule.rationale: Testing low-risk assumptions wastes discovery budget. An assumption that is easy to test but low-risk should be accepted as true and built on, not tested.

rule.statement: For each experiment, write the hypothesis as: "If we do X, we expect Y in Z% of [user segment]." Define success criteria before running the experiment.
rule.rationale: Post-hoc success criteria are subject to confirmation bias. Defining them before running eliminates the temptation to reinterpret results.

rule.statement: Timebox discovery to 1–4 weeks. Discovery that takes longer than the build it is meant to de-risk has inverted the cost equation.
rule.rationale: Endless discovery is a form of avoidance. A 4-week discovery timebox forces prioritization to the highest-risk assumptions and prevents "let's just do one more interview."

rule.statement: After experiments: if all critical risks are addressed, proceed to delivery. If a critical risk is invalidated, pivot or kill. If results are unclear, run one more targeted experiment — not another full discovery cycle.
rule.rationale: The decision categories prevent the most common discovery failure: inconclusive results that produce neither a green light nor a kill signal, leaving the team stuck.

example.note: 4 assumptions tested across value, feasibility, and business risk. Decision: proceed with MVP after full validation.
example.code:
Opportunity: AI-powered writing assistant
Assumptions tested: | Assumption | Risk Type | Test | Result | |------------|-----------|------|--------| | Users struggle with writing | Value | 10 customer interviews | Validated (8/10 confirmed problem) | | Users would use AI for help | Value | Fake door test (banner link) | 15% click-through (threshold: 10%) — validated | | We can build accurate AI | Feasibility | Technical spike, 2 days | Possible, requires 2 months integration work | | Users will pay $10/month | Business | Pricing survey (n=50) | $10/mo acceptable to 60% of respondents — validated |
Decision: Proceed to MVP, 4-week delivery target.

example.note: 4 assumptions tested. Decision: proceed with localization plan — assumptions validated but scope is larger than initial estimate.
example.code:
Opportunity: Launch product in Germany
Assumptions tested: | Assumption | Risk Type | Test | Result | |------------|-----------|------|--------| | Same product works without changes | Value | 5 DE customer interviews | Needs localization (strings, date formats, GDPR) | | Existing payment methods work | Feasibility | Technical research | Need SEPA + Klarna — 3 weeks extra | | Support team can handle German | Business | Team assessment | Need 1 German-speaking support hire | | Market size justifies effort | Business | Market research | €5M SAM in target segment — viable |
Decision: Proceed with localization plan, 8-week timeline (not 4 as originally assumed).

antipattern.description: Designing experiments to confirm the idea rather than test it — asking leading questions, selecting friendly users, setting easy success thresholds.
antipattern.reason: Confirmation bias produces validated assumptions that are actually false. Ask "what would have to be true for this to fail?" and design the experiment to find that.

antipattern.description: Running only customer interviews without behavioral or quantitative validation.
antipattern.reason: Interviews capture what users say; analytics and experiments capture what they do. Users often say they will use a feature and do not. Combine methods for confidence.

antipattern.description: Discovery drags on past 4 weeks without a clear decision.
antipattern.reason: Endless discovery is avoidance in disguise. After 4 weeks you have enough signal to make a decision: proceed, pivot, or kill. "Let's do one more interview" at week 6 is not discovery — it is delay.

antipattern.description: Team begins building features during the discovery phase.
antipattern.reason: Building during discovery means discoveries that invalidate the idea arrive after significant investment. Keep experiments cheap; if you are building, you have already decided to proceed.

antipattern.description: Learnings from experiments are not documented or shared — they exist only in the PM's head.
antipattern.reason: Undocumented learnings evaporate. The same assumptions are re-tested in future cycles by different team members. Write an experiment report for every test, even failed ones.

template.purpose: Discovery kickoff document with context, team, questions, assumptions, success criteria
Experiment hypothesis, setup, results, learnings, and go/no-go decision
